summary:
The Marshall Plan, formally known as the European Recovery Program, was a U.S. initiative launched in 1948 that provided approximately $13 billion in economic assistance (equivalent to roughly $140 billion today) to help rebuild Western European economies devastated by [[event:112|World War II]]. Named after Secretary of State George C. Marshall, who proposed it in a June 1947 speech at Harvard University, the program operated from 1948 to 1952 and directed aid to sixteen Western European nations including the United Kingdom, France, West Germany, Italy, and the Netherlands.

The plan had both humanitarian and strategic motivations. Western Europe faced food shortages, industrial collapse, and massive infrastructure damage. There were also deep fears in Washington that economic desperation would drive voters toward communist parties, which were strong in France and Italy in the immediate postwar years. Soviet expansion into Eastern Europe through the installation of communist governments reinforced the urgency. The Marshall Plan was thus simultaneously a tool of reconstruction and a weapon in the early [[event:113|Cold War]].

The Soviet Union rejected participation and pressured its Eastern European satellites to do the same. Stalin viewed the program as economic imperialism designed to create Western European dependence on the United States and extend American influence across the continent. His refusal to participate and his blocking of Eastern European participation widened the division of Europe into two economic blocs.

The program produced remarkable results in Western Europe. By 1952, participating economies had surpassed their prewar levels of industrial and agricultural production. The aid funded not only reconstruction but also modernization of infrastructure and industrial equipment, helping lay the foundations for the postwar European economic miracle. West Germany's participation after 1949, channeled through what became the Federal Republic, was particularly significant for European recovery.

The Marshall Plan also contributed to European political integration by requiring recipient nations to cooperate in distributing aid through the Organisation for European Economic Co-operation (OEEC), a precursor to the [[event:115|European Union]]. The collaborative framework it created helped normalize inter-European economic relations and established habits of multilateral cooperation that shaped subsequent European integration.
